questions.question: What does a successful CAS (compare-and-swap) operation guarantee about the target memory location?
options: The location has never been modified by any thread
The location's current value matches the caller's expected value at the moment of the swap
The location will not be modified again until the next fence
All threads agree the location holds the new value in the total global order
questions.explanation: CAS checks that *addr == expected *at the instant of the atomic operation*. If true, it stores new and returns success. It guarantees nothing about the history before or after — only that the expected value was present when the swap happened.

questions.question: The ABA problem occurs when:
options: A thread modifies a shared variable three times in succession
A CAS operation succeeds because the pointer's bit pattern matches, even though the memory was freed and reallocated in between
Two threads deadlock while trying to acquire the same mutex
A fetch_add operation returns an unexpected value due to memory reordering
questions.explanation: The ABA problem: the shared value changes from A to B and back to A. A concurrent CAS that only checks for A (the bit pattern) cannot distinguish 'nothing happened' from 'something happened and was then undone.'

questions.question: A tagged pointer prevents the ABA problem by:
options: Using a garbage collector to defer memory reclamation
Halting all threads before every CAS operation
Embedding a version counter in unused pointer bits and incrementing it on every successful CAS
Storing the previous value in a separate log that is checked before every CAS
questions.explanation: A tagged pointer co-locates a monotonic version counter in unused pointer bits (e.g., the low 3 bits which are always 0 due to alignment). Every successful CAS increments the tag. Even if a pointer address cycles back to a previously used value, the tag differs, so CAS fails and the algorithm retries with the current (correct) head.

questions.question: Given 8 threads each calling fetch_add(1, Relaxed) on the same AtomicUsize 1 million times, which statement is true?
options: The final value is always exactly 8,000,000, but some increments may be invisible to other threads until a SeqCst fence is issued
The final value is always exactly 8,000,000 because fetch_add is atomic and monotonic — each increment produces a unique result
The final value may be less than 8,000,000 because Relaxed ordering allows the writes to be lost
The final value is undefined because concurrent writes to the same atomic cause a data race
questions.explanation: fetch_add is atomic and each call returns a unique value in the sequence. Relaxed ordering is sufficient for a counter because there is no cross-thread dependency on the timing of the increment. The total count will always be 8,000,000 (the join guarantees all threads have completed).

questions.question: What is the Compare-And-Swap (CAS) operation?
options: A way to sort data atomically
An atomic operation that updates a memory location only if its current value matches an expected value
A method for swapping two variables
A network protocol for data exchange
questions.explanation: CAS(addr, expected, new) atomically: if *addr == expected, set *addr = new and return true; otherwise return false. This is the foundation of lock-free data structures — you can optimistically compute a new value and retry if someone else changed the memory in the meantime.
